text:
Cities and Urban Frontiers: Alternative Geographies of Cultural Policy (Studies in Urban Ethnography) Book Description This volume advances a new theoretical approach to urban cultural policy by centering place at the heart of cultural policy inquiry. Contesting the longstanding global emphasis on national and international frameworks of policy, the contributors to this groundbreaking volume make a compelling case for conceiving of cultural policy as a form of emplaced, embodied, and situated practice. Employing frameworks drawn from geography, political science, heritage studies, and public administration, among other fields, the diverse range of contributors focus on the “local” not simply as a geographical site but rather as a place of struggle, politics, and practice.

ArtPlace USA invited Urban Repertories to develop a volume of papers focused on the concept of the local within the context of the increasingly globalized field of cultural policy. This project was organized into three sections of papers representing the Disciplining, Managing and Practising the local. The first section aims to help understand how to ‘discipline’ the local so that the boundaries and territories that make up the geographical space can be delineated and recognised, while also acknowledging the multiple meanings and values that exist beyond fixed administrative borders. In the second section, the management of the local is explored through the analysis of various institutional instruments and programmes used to foster local development including municipal cultural plans in the USA, community asset transfers in the UK and regional industrial strategies. The third section includes case studies of practices of the local taken from across the globe and representing a wide range of different activities from the reclamation of former silos for public art in rural Cretan villages and silo art trails in regional Australia to the engagement of community-based participatory policy research in Cape Town and arts development projects in the Irish urban fringe.

The contributors to this collection illuminate how the local is often used as a Trojan horse to disguise national concerns and at the same time how local communities appropriate, reformulate and resist globalizing, professionalized modes of policy-making. The collection reconceptualizes the local as a pivotal site in cultural policy scholarship and in so doing illuminates how cultural value is made and circulated in the everyday contexts of particular local environments. The collection thus argues that an effective cultural policy has to be highly attuned to the very different social and spatial conditions of place.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
